summary: Gruber argues that Apple's new iPad Air and iPad Mini are best understood not as updates to their namesakes but as repositioned products: the Air is essentially an updated 10.5-inch iPad Pro, and the Mini is a smaller version of the same. He clarifies that the low-cost iPad role now belongs to the $329 9.7-inch model, while the Mini has become a full-fledged peer to the Air. The bulk of the post addresses why these iPads use the original Apple Pencil instead of the Pencil 2, explaining that the Pencil 2's magnetic charging requires flat-sided industrial design that Apple integrates holistically with edge-to-edge displays and Face ID. Gruber concludes that Apple chose a messy product lineup over a messy product.
key_points: The new iPad Air is functionally an updated 10.5-inch iPad Pro, not a successor to the old Air, and the Mini is simply a smaller version of the same device
The iPad Mini has shed its role as the low-cost iPad — that position now belongs to the $329 9.7-inch iPad
The Pencil 2's magnetic charging requires flat sides, which Apple ties to an integrated design language including edge-to-edge displays and Face ID that would raise costs
Apple's philosophy is to keep individual products clean even if it means a confusing product lineup — a messy lineup beats a messy product
money_quotes: The new iPad Air isn't based on the old iPad Air — it's an update to the 10.5-inch iPad Pro.
The new Mini is really just a smaller version of the new iPad Air.
When it debuted in 2012, the iPad Mini was both the small iPad and the low-cost iPad.
The new iPad Mini is a full-fledged peer to the new iPad Air technically. It's all about the size.
Such design elements are integrated with the whole.
Better to have a messy product lineup where some new iPads only support the new Pencil and others only support the old Pencil than to have a messy new Pencil.
key_insight: Apple would rather have a confusing product lineup than compromise the design integrity of any individual product.
